sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section II, List of Answers on Topics 69:1

items.sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section II, List of Answers on Topics 69:1
items.html: Because the upper light is always in a state of complete rest, without any innovation of form. In spirituality, innovation is regarded as movement, and any innovation of form that is done as an expansion of the upper light is through the force of extension that is revealed only in the emanated being (See Part One, answer 64). Even this expansion of upper light is like lighting one candle from another, while the first is not lessened whatsoever. Thus, only that part of the expansion of the upper light that the emanated being received is regarded as receiving a new form, a common relation between the vessel and the light that is clothed in it. However, the upper light itself is not lessened at all, and noting is innovated in it by this expansion that has reached the emanated being.
